segments.s201-g105: This phrase strings descriptive nouns/adjectives in classical word order: برادر (addressed as “brother”) + سرافراز (an attributive adjective “proud/triumphant”) + جنگی (another adjective “warlike/fighter”). فرود here is a nominal/participial sense (“descent/appearance” — ‘appeared’), placed after the modifiers in poetic syntax to complete the picture.
segments.s201-g106: کجا ... نبود is a rhetorical negative question meaning “where was there …?”; هم is an intensifier 'even', چنو = 'such', در زمانه = 'in the age/that time', and نبود is the past form 'was not' (there was not). Together: “Nowhere in that age was there anyone like him.”
segments.s201-g107: بکشتی is the classical past 2nd-person form 'you killed' (ب- prefix + کشتی). کسی را is the definite/accusative phrase 'someone' marked by را, and که introduces the relative clause 'who/that'; در کارزار sets the location 'in the battlefield.'
segments.s201-g108: چو here is temporal 'when/whenever'; تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'; لشکری خواستی is past 'you wanted troops' (لشکری = 'an army/troops' as object, خواستی = 'you desired/wished'); روزکار ('the day's work' / 'affairs of the day') gives the purpose/context — i.e. for the work of war or campaign.
segments.s201-g109: وزان پس (وز + آن پس) is a compounded temporal/discourse link 'and from that / after that'; که رفتی introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when you went' (رفتی = past 'you went'); بران is an archaic demonstrative/adverb 'to that place' and رزمگاه = 'battlefield'.
segments.s201-g110: نبودت is نبود + the 2nd-person suffix ت meaning 'there was not for you' / 'you had not'; به جز = 'except'; رامش = 'tranquility/rest' and بزمگاه = 'place of revelry/banquet'. The clause says you were left with nothing except rest and feasting-place.
segments.s201-g111: ترا جایگه نیست literally arranges as 'to you a place is not' (ترا = 'for you' / object/dative, جایگه = 'place/rank', نیست = 'is not'); در شارستان specifies location 'in the town/city' — i.e. 'You have no place in the city.'
segments.s201-g112: بزیبد is a classical verbal form meaning 'it befits / suits' or 'becomes'; the logical reading is that بند و بیمارستان (fetters and the hospital) are the things that suit you. ترا (again the 2nd-person marker) is the logical object/ beneficiary: 'fetters and hospital befit you.'
segments.s201-g121: برو is an imperative 'go' addressing the addressee. جاودان modifies خانه ("eternal house"); the phrase خانه زندان توست is the predicate 'the house is your prison' with the possessive pronoun تو placed before the copula ست for emphasis. ست is the copula 'is' attached at the end to complete the clause.
segments.s201-g122: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back; گوهر بد is a noun + adjective phrase 'evil jewel' (گوهر = jewel, بد = bad/evil). نگهبان توست is a predicate 'is your guard' where تو ('your/you') precedes the copula ست, and the whole clause describes the thing that watches or guards the addressee.
segments.s201-g123: ز پیشش = 'from before him / out of his presence' with ز as the poetic preposition 'from' and پیشش = 'before him' (ش = his). براند is a past/preterite-like verbal form 'drove away' (3rd singular). و links clauses, and بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said' that introduces a quoted command; the following بند here functions as the quoted imperative/command 'bind' (or refers to a fetter) in the reported speech.
segments.s201-g124: به بند is an adverbial phrase 'bond by bond / link by link' formed with به + noun بند; از دلش = 'from his heart' (دل + possessive clitic ش = his). بیخ شادی = 'the root of joy' (بیخ = root, modifying شادی). بکند is the subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form used after a verb of commanding (here reporting the command) meaning 'let them / should they do' or simply 'they should do / cause' the action described (i.e., pluck out or strip away).
segments.s201-g17: This is a negative present/prefactual clause: نیابد is the classical 3rd-person-present (or subjunctive/prefactual) of یابیدن used here to mean “does not obtain / does not find.” Word order is emphatic: the object/goal رهایی (release) appears first, then the verb نیابد, and the nominal سر (head) follows as the theme that cannot be freed; از بند specifies the source/constraint (“from the bond/fetter”). اوی is a possessive pronoun attached after بند and here means “his.”
segments.s201-g18: یکی را is an indirect-object/topic phrase (“to/for one person”); را is the definite/object marker. همه فر و اورند is a coordination of two nouns (all the فر “divine/royal glory” and اورند); the final possessive اوی (‘his’) attaches to the last noun but scope-wise applies to the whole coordinated phrase (a common Persian pattern: a single possessive can govern the whole coordinated noun phrase).
segments.s201-g19: یکی را دگر places یکی را (“to one/someone” with the object marker) before دگر (“another/other”), yielding the sense “to another person.” شوربختی is a direct object meaning “misfortune.” دهد is the 3rd-person singular present/simple classical verb ‘to give/inflict’ used here to mean ‘he causes/inflicts.’
segments.s201-g20: This is a simple list of objects linked by و (and): نیاز و غم و درد و سختی (“need, sorrow, pain, and hardship”), followed by the same verb دهد (ellipsis of explicit subject: the same actor as the previous clause). Each noun is given in bare form as a coordinated accusative; the verb repeats the sense ‘gives/inflicts.’
segments.s201-g21: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from.” This phrase gives a range: رخشنده خورشید (the radiant/shining sun) تا تیره خاک (to the dark earth); تا here marks the endpoint of the range (“as far as/to”). Adjectival words (رخشنده, تیره) modify the following nouns in the expected order.
segments.s201-g22: همه داد is a noun phrase meaning “all (forms of) justice”; بینم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن used here as the poet’s ‘I see’ or ‘I behold.’ ز یزدان پاک is a source phrase (“from the pure God”); note پاک is a postpositive adjective modifying یزدان here. The clause thus expresses perception of divine justice across the stated range.
segments.s201-g23: بشد is an archaic/epic past-ish verb meaning “it came to be / he became / he appeared” (verb placed before subject is common in poetry). طوس is the subject (the hero Tūs). با + noun (با کاویانی درفش) is an instrumental/associative phrase “with the Kayanian banner/standard.” The clause thus says Tūs acted/appeared accompanied by that banner.
segments.s201-g24: ز here means “from/of” indicating origin or composition (he came from an army). لشکر چهل مرد is a genitive/partitive group “an army of forty men.” زرینه کفش is a nominal phrase where the adjective زرینه (“golden”) modifies کفش (“shoes”); the whole phrase describes those forty men as wearing golden shoes.
segments.s201-g89: برفت is a past 3rd‑person singular verb meaning ‘he went off’ (the prefixed ب- is a conventional poetic/older form). و simply coordinates the two verbs. ببرد here is a past action ‘he took/carry away’ (parallel to برفت). آنک is a relative/relative-like particle ‘that which’ and is followed by the noun phrase بد نوذری ‘ill-omened votive (offering)’, which qualifies the ‘that’ (the whole relative phrase is the object of ببرد).
segments.s201-g90: This is a coordinated noun phrase in apposition to the previous object: سواران 'horsemen' followed by the epithet جنگ‌آور 'war‑bringing/warrior' and و linking the second noun/adjective لشکری. In classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking is often omitted, so these words pile up as modifiers/coordination: 'the warlike horsemen and the soldiery.'
segments.s201-g91: بنزدیک is an adverbial phrase meaning 'near, close at hand' and modifies آمد, the past 3rd‑person 'he came'. از introduces the origin/place phrase 'from the plain of battle' (دشت جنگ = 'field/plain of battle'). Word order is typical: adverb + subject (implied) + آمد + place phrase.
segments.s201-g92: بره‌بر is a simile/epithet meaning 'porter/carrier' (here used metaphorically). نکرد is the past negative 'did not do' (ن fused to کرد). ایچ‌گونه is an intensifying adverb 'in no such way/by no means' and درنگ means 'a pause/delay' — together the clause says he did not, like a porter, remain or delay at all.
segments.s201-g93: زمین را is the definite object marked with را ('the ground'). ببوسید is the past 3rd‑person 'he kissed' (poetic prefixed form). در پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the king' (در + پیش = before/in the presence of + شاه).
segments.s201-g94: نکرد is the past negative 'did not do'; ایچ (here) functions as an emphatic negation 'at all/anything' and intensifies the negation. خسرو is the subject ('Khosrow' / the king), بدو means 'toward him' (to him), and در نگاه 'in (the) look/glance' means 'with even a glance' — together: 'Khosrow did not, in any way, look at him.'
segments.s201-g95: بدشنام is an epithet 'the ill‑famed / notorious one' serving as the subject. بگشاد is a past action 'he opened/unloosed' (poetic prefixed form of گشودن). لب شهریار is a possessive juxtaposition 'the king's lip/mouth' (no ezāfe in this poetic line) meaning 'the king’s speech' or 'the king’s mouth' — so the clause says the ill‑famed man made the king speak.
segments.s201-g96: بران functions adverbially 'thereby / upon that'; انجمن is 'assembly/meeting' and طوس is the personal name Tūs (object of the action) marked by را. کرد is the causative/past 'made' and خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised/lowly' — 'thereby he made Tūs despised before that assembly.'
segments.s201-g9: بنام is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning "in the name of" and introduces the following noun phrase. خداوند is the head noun 'Lord/God' and خورشید و ماه are two coordinated nouns in apposition to that title ('the Lord of the Sun and the Moon'); the conjunction و simply joins خورشید and ماه.
segments.s201-g10: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. داد is a past tense verb here meaning 'gave/placed' (an older usage — simple past). بر is the preposition 'upon/on', which introduces its object نیکوی دستگاه; نیکوی is an archaic adjectival form 'good/fair' placed before the noun (poetic word order), and دستگاه is 'system/order' — so the clause asks where He established/gave justice over the fair order.
segments.s201-g11: This is a simple past-tense clause: three coordinated nouns (جهان 'world', مکان 'place', زمان 'time') joined by و, followed by the past verb آفرید 'created'. The subject is implicit (God/the one previously invoked).
segments.s201-g12: پی here functions adverbially ('after/next' or 'in succession' / 'also'), introducing another list of creations. مور 'ant' and پیل 'elephant' are coordinated with و; گران is an adjective 'heavy/large' modifying پیل (post-nominal adjective), and آفرید is the past verb 'created'. The construction lists both tiny and huge creatures as created things.
segments.s201-g13: ازویست is a fused, archaic form meaning 'it is from him' (از وی است). پیروزی 'victory' is the noun governed by that phrase. The conjunction و links the next phrase; ز (poetic form of از) + و (here the coordinating vowel/connector) again means 'from him', and شکیب is an archaic noun 'patience/endurance'. The whole segment states that victory and patience originate from him.
segments.s201-g14: بنیک is an archaic adjectival/adverbial form meaning 'good' or 'with goodness'. The paired phrase بنیک و ببد is a correlative (roughly 'for good or for ill' / 'good and bad'); ببد is an archaic past-form element (literary), and the following زو ('from him') marks source. رسد is present/future-like in poetic usage meaning 'comes / is attained' (here 'becomes/arrives'), taking کام 'desire' as its object; و زیب adds 'and beauty'. Overall: whether good or ill, from him come desire (fulfillment) and beauty.
segments.s201-g15: خرد داد is a verb-object phrase: خرد 'wisdom' as direct object and داد 'gave' (past). The conjunction و links to جان و تن زورمند: 'جان' (soul/life) and 'تن' (body) are coordinated, and the adjective زورمند 'powerful/strong' follows and applies to them (post-nominal adjective), so the line says he gave wisdom and a strong soul and body.
segments.s201-g16: This is a noun list describing further endowments: بزرگی 'greatness' (noun), و linking to دیهیم — an archaic word (literary) glossed here as 'dihim' ('rural' / 'of the land/people') used poetically and somewhat ambiguous in exact nuance — and then و تخت بلند where تخت 'throne' is modified by the adjective بلند 'high/lofty' (adjective after noun as normal). The segment enumerates grandeur, a certain popular/rustic quality, and a high throne.
segments.s201-g1: This is a simple transitive clause: object (دبیر خردمند) + definite object marker را + prepositional/adverbial پیش + past tense verb خواند. را marks the preceding noun as the definite direct object. پیش خواند here means 'called/summoned into his presence' (پیش = in the presence of; خواند = called/summoned). The adjective خردمند follows the noun (typical Persian order: noun + adjective).
segments.s201-g2: This is a nominal clause with a verbal copula plus a following verb: دل آگنده بودش ز غم (his heart was filled with grief) + برفشاند. بودش is بود + enclitic -ش (here functioning like 'it was' or 'was his/it was'), so the clause literally reads 'the heart was full from grief.' برفشاند is a following verb meaning 'scattered/let fall' and here is used figuratively (i.e. shedding or scattering tears); the subject is linked to the preceding phrase. Note the poetic, somewhat compressed word order and the enclitic on بود that refers back to the heart.
segments.s201-g3: یکی here functions as an indefinite/numberal modifier ('one' / 'a certain') modifying نامه. بنوشت is an archaic/pseudo-prefixal past form of نوشت (he wrote) often found in epic diction (the b- prefix appears in older/formal pasts). پر آب چشم is a noun phrase meaning 'full of water/tears in the eyes' (پر = full (of); آب = water/tears figuratively; چشم = eye). Word order is noun (نامه) then verb (بنوشت) then a following adjectival/qualifying phrase.
segments.s201-g4: ز بهر = 'for the sake of / because of / on account of' (ز = from/because of; بهر = for). برادر is the beneficiary/target ('his brother'). پر از درد و خشم is a follow-up adjectival phrase meaning 'full of pain and anger' (پر = full (of); از introduces the thing it is full of). The phrase as a whole expresses motive/recipient and the emotional content of the letter.
segments.s201-g5: بسوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' or 'to.' The sequence فریبرز کاوس شاه is an appositional/onomastic string: proper name (فریبرز) followed by the father's/name/title (کاوس شاه) — a compact epic way of specifying the addressee. The preposition governs the whole phrase.
segments.s201-g6: یکی سوی = 'one (in) the direction (to)' — here marking that another letter or dispatch goes to a different addressee. پرمایگان سپاه is a genitive/nominal compound: سپاه (host, army) qualified by پرمایگان ('the great ones' / nobles), i.e. 'the host of the nobles.' Word order places the directional phrase before the recipient noun phrase.
segments.s201-g7: سر نامه ('the head/opening of the letter') is a nominal phrase in which سر = 'opening/head' qualifies نامه. بود is the past copula 'was' (here 'was/consisted of'), and از + نخست آفرین functions as a prepositional phrase 'from/of the First‑Creator' — i.e. the letter opens with praise to the First Creator. This is an idiomatic epic formula: 'it began with (praise of) the First Creator.'
segments.s201-g8: چنانچون = 'just as / in the way that.' بود is the past copula 'was.' رسم آیین و دین is a noun phrase meaning 'the custom/practice of ritual and religion' (رسم = custom; آیین = ritual/ceremony; و = and; دین = religion). The clause says the opening is exactly in accordance with religious/ceremonial custom.
segments.s201-g49: هنرها (plural) is the subject 'virtues/skills' followed by همه reinforcing 'all/everyone' — the order 'هنرها همه' is emphatic. هست is the copula 'are/is' used here with a plural subject in a classical/poetic register. نزدیک is the predicative adjective 'near', and اوی is the old third-person enclitic pronoun meaning 'him' (dative/locative here): together they mean 'are near him.'
segments.s201-g50: مبادا introduces a negative wish or preventative clause 'may it not be / lest'. چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'so/such' modifying the following noun phrase. جان تاریک اوی is 'his dark soul' (جان = 'soul/life', تاریک = 'dark/gloomy'); اوی here is possessive/determiner 'his.' The whole clause expresses a wish that his soul not be thus dark.
segments.s201-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. این نامه خوانی is a second-person present/future clause 'you read this letter' (خوانی is the 2nd-person present form). هم‌اندر is an adverbial/archaic compound 'also/together/at the same time', and شتاب ('haste') qualifies the manner — i.e. 'read this letter in haste' or 'when you read... hurry.'
segments.s201-g52: This is an imperative instruction built around the verb phrase 'دور کن' — ز + دل = 'from the heart', دور 'far/distant' + کن (imperative 'make') = 'put/make (it) far from the heart' = 'drive from your heart'. خورد here is an adjectival/adverbial 'a little / small' modifying آرام و خواب ('calm and sleep'), so the sense is 'drive even a little calm and sleep from your heart.'
segments.s201-g53: سبک طوس here is a proper name (Sabak-e Tus, the messenger), so سبک functions as a name followed by طوس (Tus). را is the definite object marker. بازگردان is an imperative/infinitival command 'return/restore/give back'. بجای means 'to the place / back to his place.' The phrase commands that Sabak of Tus be sent back to where he belongs.
segments.s201-g54: ز فرمان = 'from the command/order' (ز = from, فرمان = command). مگرد here is an archaic negative imperative 'do not turn (away)' or 'do not deviate' (the dictionary senses shown are for the conjunction مگر; as used here it is a verbal negative), so the clause means 'do not turn away from the order.' و connects the next prohibition; مزن is a negative imperative 'do not (offer/speak/strike)' used idiomatically with رای 'opinion/counsel' — 'مزن هیچ رای' = 'offer no counsel / give no opinion.'
segments.s201-g55: سپهدار و سالار are two coordinating titles 'commander and chief/lord' (joined by و). زرینه is an adjective 'golden,' modifying کفش 'shoe(s)/boots' following it, so the phrase describes the apparel or status: 'commander and chief with golden shoes/boots.'
segments.s201-g56: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'. می‌باش is the classical present/imperative form of 'to be' (with the prefix می indicating the present), here functioning as an exhortation/prediction 'you shall be / be'. با is the preposition 'with', linking to کاویانی درفش 'the Kayanian/Kayanid standard/banner' (کاویانی adjectival/attributive). The whole means 'you are/be with the Kayanian banner.'
segments.s201-g73: بنزد (prepositional phrase) precedes the name and means 'to the presence of / to' (preposition + noun). فریبرز is the proper name (the addressee). شد is the past auxiliary/verb here meaning 'came/arrived' (classical Persian uses شد for a past occurrence). نامه‌دار is a compound 'نامه' + دار ‘-holder’ = 'letter-bearer' (دار functions as 'holder' here; that sense is lexicalized in compounds). The whole clause is V (شد) after a locative/prepositional phrase, producing 'to Fereyberz came the letter-bearer.'
segments.s201-g74: بدو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him' (به + او collapsed), placed before the verb. داد is the past tense 'gave'. پس is an adverb 'then' (marks sequence). نامه شهریار is a juxtaposed genitive: 'the king's letter' (noun + possessor). Word order here is 'to-him gave then the king's letter' = 'then gave him the king's letter.'
segments.s201-g75: فریبرز is the subject (he). طوس و یلان را is the direct object phrase marked by را (را makes the following noun phrase definite/accusative): 'Tus and the heroes.' بخواند here is the classical/poetic past-tense sense 'he called/summoned' (the b- prefix appears in narrative/poetic forms).
segments.s201-g76: ز is the poetic preposition 'about / concerning' (introducing the matter being spoken of). کار گذشته is a genitive phrase 'past deed(s)/affairs' (گذشته postmodifies کار). فراوان is an adverbial/qualifier 'at length / abundantly' modifying the verb. براند is the verb; in context it functions as the predicator 'he related / brought forth' in classical narrative. (Note: the dictionary senses supplied list only 'drive/drive away' for براند; here it is used poetically to mean 'recount/relate', so that specific sense is not among the provided options.)
segments.s201-g77: همان نامور is an emphatic epithet: 'that very renowned / famed.' گیو و گودرز are proper names joined by و 'and.' را is the definite-object marker attaching to the noun phrase, signaling these persons are objects of the verb in the preceding clause.
segments.s201-g78: سواران و گردان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the horsemen and the troop(s)'. آن مرز is a genitive/complement 'of that frontier' (آن modifies مرز). را at the end marks the whole noun phrase as definite/direct object (one را can scope over the coordinated nouns).
segments.s201-g79: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' برخواند is the classical poetic past-tense verb here meaning 'read aloud / recited' (used for reading a letter). آن نامه شهریار is the definite noun phrase 'that letter of the king' as the object of reading.
segments.s201-g80: جهان را places 'the world' as the affected sphere (را marks it definite/accusative; fronting it gives 'upon the world'). درختی نو is an indefinite noun phrase 'a new tree' (درختی = 'a tree', نو = 'new'). آمد is the past 'came' (a standard narrative past). ببار here is an archaic/poetic verb element meaning roughly 'to spring/produce/come forth' in this construction; its exact sense ('to rain / pour') in the supplied list does not match the poetic usage, so that sense is not available in the provided options.
segments.s201-g33: کنون is an adverb meaning “now.” بر + برادر is a prepositional phrase meaning “for/over the brother” (in classical Persian بر can mean “over/for”), placed before the predicate for emphasis. بباید is an impersonal modal construction (“one must/ought to”) and is followed by the verbal form گریست (here used as the action ‘to weep’), producing the impersonal sense “one must weep.”
segments.s201-g34: ندانم is the negative present/past-stem form meaning “I do not (or I did not) know” (classical 3rd/1st‑person forms are flexible in the epic). مرا is the topical/benefactive pronoun (here written as two pieces in the gloss), marking that the ignorance is about what concerns me; را is functioning as a topic/emphasis marker attached to that pronoun. دشمن و دوست کیست is a nominal question clause “who is enemy and (who) is friend,” with کی (who) and the copula ست (is) at the end.
segments.s201-g35: مرو here is the imperative ‘Do not go’ (the same form can also be the place-name Merv, but context makes the negative imperative clear). گفتم is the speaker’s verb ‘I said’ (classical past first person) and is placed after the quoted imperative in the poet’s narrative order; او را is the indirect object ‘to him’ (را marking the person addressed). براه is the compound prepositional phrase ‘toward / on the road to’ and is followed by چرم, here a place-name or destination in the phrase (“toward Chirm/Charm”), so the whole segment reports “I said to him, ‘Do not go toward Chirm.’ ”
segments.s201-g36: مزن is the prohibitive imperative ‘do not strike’ (mizan → ma-zan in classical orthography). بر introduces the target of the action (‘upon / against’), کلات and سپدکوه are place names (Kalat; Sepd‑kuh), joined by و, and دم here means ‘edge/side’ or ‘at the foot/edge’ of the mountain. The phrase therefore orders not to strike at those places or at the mountain’s edge.
segments.s201-g37: بران here is a locative/adverbial particle ‘on/thereon’ (’on that’), followed by ره ‘way/road’ to yield ‘on that road.’ فرودست is an adjective ‘lowly/subordinate’ used predicatively. The conjunction و links to با لشکرست: با + لشکر + -ست (copula) gives the sense ‘and there is/they are with a host,’ so the segment describes the condition of those on that road and that they are with a host.
segments.s201-g38: همان is a demonstrative ‘the same / that very (one).’ کی introduces a relative/interrogative ‘who’ and pairs with نژاد است to state ‘who is of such lineage’ (نژاد = lineage). The following و connects to کنداور است, where کنداور is an agent‑noun (literally ‘digger/excavator’) and است is the copula ‘is.’ Together this identifies ‘that same one’ as both of that lineage and an excavator/agent of the named action.
segments.s201-g39: نداند is the negative present/past-stem ‘does not know’ (classical 3rd‑person form). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that/what,’ and این لشکر is the subject ‘this army/host.’ از بن کیند is a verbal phrase: از بن ‘from the root/foundation’ and کیند is an archaic third‑person plural verb form (‘they dig’ or ‘are sprung’/‘they do’ in context). The clause asks/notes ignorance about the army’s origin (‘from what root this army springs’).
segments.s201-g40: This short clause continues the thought: از ایران ‘if/from Iran’ (locative/original phrase), سپاهند‌گر is an agent‑noun meaning ‘army‑leader/commander,’ خود is the reflexive/possessive ‘his own,’ and چیند is the present/archaic verb ‘he gathers/plucks/collects.’ The literal sequence ‘from Iran the commander his‑own gathers’ should be read as ‘if from Iran, the commander will muster/gather his own (troops).’
segments.s201-g25: بتوران is a poetic locative/directional phrase (here equivalent to به توران, 'to Turan') placed before the verb; فرستادمش is فرستادم + enclitic object -ش ('I sent him/it'), and با سپاه is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('with an army') following the verb. Word order is: direction — verb(+object) — instrument.
segments.s201-g26: برادر is the subject ('brother'); شد is an intransitive past/preterite 'became' (here 'was made/was rendered'); تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined'; از کین نخستین is a causal prepositional phrase ('because of the first hatred/grudge') placed between the verb and adjective to mark cause.
segments.s201-g27: بایران is a poetic locative expression equivalent to به ایران ('in Iran') placed at the start to set scope; چنو ('such') modifies the following noun phrase; هیچ مهتر ('any chief') is a negated noun phrase and مباد is the classical negative optative/jussive meaning 'may it not be' — so the whole is a wish: 'may there be no such chief in Iran.'
segments.s201-g28: وزین گونه is an adjectival phrase 'of weighty/dignified kind' placed before the head noun it qualifies; سالار لشکر is a genitive/possessive compound 'commander of the army'; مباد repeats the negative jussive 'may there not be', so the clause continues the same optative wish about a commander of that kind.
segments.s201-g29: دریغا is an interjection 'Alas!'; برادر is the vocative/subject 'my brother'; فرود (here a noun/participial notion 'fall' / 'fallen') followed by جوان ('young' or 'young man') attributes youth to the fallen one. The phrase succinctly says the brother fell while young.
segments.s201-g30: سر نامداران is a nominal compound where سر ('head/chief') takes نامداران ('the renowned/illustrious ones') as its complement, meaning 'the chief among the famed'; و simply links the two coordinated roles; پشت گوان uses پشت ('back/support') with گوان ('of/like them') to indicate he was the support/pillar of his peers. Both phrases are appositive/descriptive of the brother.
segments.s201-g31: ز کین پدر is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of (the) father's hatred' (ز here = 'because of'); زار و گریان are predicative adjectives/participles 'wretched and weeping' describing the speaker's state; بدم in this archaic context functions as a first‑person copula/verb 'I was/I became' (not captured by the provided sense list).
segments.s201-g32: بران ('thereon' / 'for that') introduces the consequence; درد is 'sorrow/pain' (emotional here); یک چند is an adverbial phrase 'for a while/several (a short time)'; بریان is a predicative adjective 'consumed/burned (figuratively, by grief)'; بدم again is the first‑person predicate 'I was/I became' in archaic usage (sense not listed).
segments.s201-g81: A coordinated noun phrase: two nouns joined by و (and). The second noun شیران is figurative here ('brave men' = warriors). ایران‌زمین is a postnominal place-phrase modifying both nouns (the expected ezāfe is omitted for poetic meter), so the whole means 'the great ones and warriors of the land of Iran.'
segments.s201-g82: Main clause with compact, slightly inverted word order: همه (subject 'all') + شاه را (definite object marked by را) + خواندند (past 3pl 'they called/uttered') followed by the exclamation آفرین which is the content of their call. را here marks the king as the definite focus of the action; آفرین is an interjection meaning 'bravo.'
segments.s201-g83: Verb-first poetic order: بیاورد (biyāvard) is a third-person past/perfective verb 'brought' (here used narratively), followed by the agent طوس (Tūs). آن گرامی درفش is a determiner + adjective + noun phrase 'that honored standard' describing the object brought.
segments.s201-g84: A noun list apposed to the previous phrase: ابا here functions as the name/title of the standard (presented without an overt copula), followed by a series of objects linked by و: کوس (war-drum), پیلان (elephants, plural), and زرینه کفش ('gilded/golden shoes' — adjective before noun). The whole is a nominative list of items brought.
segments.s201-g85: Prepositional/locative expression بنزد 'to the presence of' precedes the name فریبرز (Fariborz). بردند is past 3pl 'they brought/took (it)', with the thing brought understood from context; و گفت 'and said' introduces reported speech. Word order is compact and typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s201-g86: Reported content clause introduced by که ('that'). آمد is past 3sg 'came' used existentially ('has come'). سزا را uses را as the definite/object marker (here 'the recompense/deserved thing'); سزاوار is an adjective 'fitting/deserving' that syntactically links to جفت ('pair/spouses'), so the phrase means 'that a fitting recompense has come for the deserving pair.'
segments.s201-g87: A blessing clause: همه‌ساله 'every year' (adverbial) + بخت تو 'your fortune' (noun + possessive pronoun following the noun) + پیروز predicate 'victorious'; باد is the optative particle 'may' (a fixed wish formula in Persian). The whole reads 'May your fortune be victorious every year.'
segments.s201-g88: Parallel blessing: همه روزگار 'all (your) days/times' + تو (possessive) + نوروز as the predicate noun (used metaphorically for renewal/joy) + باد (optative 'may'). Word order places the possessor after the noun, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s201-g41: ازان کوه is a locative phrase ('from that mountain') with ازان = 'from that' + کوه = 'mountain'. جنگ آورد is a compound where جنگ is the object 'war/battle' and آورد (here causative/past) means 'brought' or 'caused'; بی‌گمان is a postpositive adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s201-g42: فراوان here works adverbially ('plentifully' / 'many') modifying the verb; سران را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker marking سران ('leaders, chiefs') as the object; سرآرد is a present/future-form (poetic) meaning 'will bring' or 'bring about'; زمان is the agent/subject 'time'. Word order is topical/poetic (adverb — object — verb — subject).
segments.s201-g43: دریغ is an exclamatory noun/interjection 'alas' or 'pity'. آنچنان is an intensifier 'so / such', and گرد in this position means 'concerning / about' (a poetic prepositional use) attaching آنچنان to خسرونژاد ('the Khosrow-born / scion of Khosrow').
segments.s201-g44: که introduces the result/clause 'that ...'. طوس is the proper name Tūs, فرومایه is an adjectival epithet 'base / low-born' modifying طوس. دادش is داد + enclitic object -ش ('gave/treated him'), and بباد is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'to the wind / cast away' — so دادش بباد = 'cast/gave him to the winds' (i.e., discarded or ruined him).
segments.s201-g45: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پیش از این is the adverbial 'before this' (پیش + از + این functioning together). او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. سپهبد بدست is a nominal phrase where بدست ('by/through') follows to indicate that the office was 'received' or 'given' — i.e., 'had been made spahbed' (poetic, verb implied or compressed).
segments.s201-g46: ز کاوس شاه is an agent phrase 'by Kāvus the king' with ز (poetic from/by). اختر بد بدست is a terse adjectival/phrase meaning 'ill-starred' (اختر 'star' + بد 'bad/unfavorable' with بدست here reinforcing the notion 'by an evil star' — a compact poetic way to say 'born under an unlucky star' or 'ill-starred').
segments.s201-g47: برزم اندرون is a locative/phrase giving location ('within the camp/inside'); نیز = 'also'. خواب is the subject 'sleep/drowsiness' and آیدش = آید + -ش 'comes to him' (verb with third-person enclitic indicating 'to him'). The verb is in the impersonal/experiential construction 'sleep comes to him'.
segments.s201-g48: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بی is the prepositional/privative 'without' (governing an understood object from context); می‌نشیند is the present/habitual third-person 'he sits down / he sits' (with the habitual/progressive prefix می). شتاب آیدش repeats the pattern 'X آیدش' ('X comes to him') with شتاب = 'haste / restlessness', so 'when he sits without (it), haste comes upon him' (i.e., he becomes restless).
segments.s201-g113: ترا (ت + را) is a topical/object clitic ('you' / 'you (as object)') placed before the locative phrase; پیش آزادگان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the free(noble) ones'; کار نیست is an existential negative phrase literally 'work is not' used idiomatically as 'there is no business/role' — together: 'You have no place/role before the nobles.'
segments.s201-g114: کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where' used with the negation نیست to produce the sense 'nowhere'; مر is the old dative/accusative 'to me' (here marking the speaker's perspective) and ترا (ت + را) again marks 'you' as the referent; رای هشیار is a noun + adjective 'wise counsel' and نیست negates existence: 'there is nowhere that your counsel is wise (i.e. your counsel is nowhere sound).' The word order is emphatic/poetic, with the datives and clitics fronted.
segments.s201-g115: سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving / worthy (of)'; it takes the following nouns without an explicit izafe in this poetic line. مسماری here denotes a nail‑studded stake/iron spikes, while بند and غل are synonyms meaning 'bonds/fetters' — the speaker lists the punishments or restraints the addressee deserves.
segments.s201-g116: نه is the negation 'not'; اندر + خور forms an idiom where خور (present stem of خوردن 'to eat/partake') with اندر indicates 'to partake of / to enjoy' something. Thus نه اندر خور تاج و دیهیم و مل means 'not fit to partake of/receive the crown, the rural patrimony, and the realm.' The nouns تاج, دیهیم, مل are coordinated objects of this verb phrase.
segments.s201-g117: نژاد منوچهر is a genitive-like phrase 'the race/lineage of Manuchehr' (poetic style omits an explicit izafe); و ریش سپید coordinates another nominative phrase 'and (a) white beard' (سپید postmodifies ریش). Together they form the subject/topic of the next clause.
segments.s201-g118: ترا + را here mark the beneficiary/object 'you'; داد is the past tense 'gave'; بر زندگانی امید is a prepositional phrase 'hope upon/for life' (بر introducing the scope or object of the giving). Word order places the object before the verb (poetic/archaic order): 'The lineage and white beard gave you hope for life.'
segments.s201-g119: وگرنه is the conditional 'otherwise'; بفرمودمی is an old/honorific past/conditional form 'I would have commanded/ordered' (بفرمود + می as modal/conditional); تا introduces the result/purpose 'so that / to the point of' and is followed by سرت 'your head' (literally 'your head', here the likely object or target implied by the command — a common poetic way to hint at execution or punishment).
segments.s201-g120: بداندیش is an adjective/noun 'ill‑minded / malicious'; کردی is the 2nd‑person past 'you did/you were' so the phrase reads 'you harbored ill intent' or 'you acted with ill intent'. جدا از برت is a prepositional phrase 'apart from / separate from برت'; برت combines the preposition بر 'upon/on' with the 2nd‑person clitic ت and here means roughly 'from your side/your lord/your support' in context — the line accuses the addressee of acting apart from his proper lord/support.
segments.s201-g65: فرازآور is a verb-form used as an imperative/command here meaning 'bring forth / raise'. از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction' with سوی functioning as 'direction/side' and هر 'every' modifying it. ساز رزم is a noun phrase 'the instrument(s) of battle' (ساز 'instrument', modified by رزم 'battle') — the ezafe linking is omitted in this poetic word order.
segments.s201-g66: مبادا is a negative jussive particle 'may it not be / lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause. آید is present/subjunctive/potential 'comes' (here 'may it come'); ت + را together function as the second‑person object clitic 'to you' (in the text split into ت and را). رای بزم is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the counsel/plan of the banquet' with رای 'counsel/plan' governed by بزم 'banquet'.
segments.s201-g67: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed / put'. از بر (poetic) marks the location 'upon / on (the breast of)' and is followed by نامه‌بر 'the letter‑carrier' as the locative phrase. مهر شاه is the object 'the king's seal' (مهر 'seal' governed by شاه 'king'). Word order is verb — locative phrase — object, typical in older poetic style.
segments.s201-g68: فرستاده را is the definite object 'to the messenger' (را as the object marker). گفت is the past tense of گفتن 'he said / told (him)'; here it introduces the reported command that follows. برکش is an imperative meaning 'take up / lift / set out' and is governed by the following adverbial براه 'onto the road / on the road'.
segments.s201-g69: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the source or starting point. رفتن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'going / travelling' governing the time expression شب و روز 'night and day' (i.e. continually). ماسای is a proper name (place or person) marking the destination or reference of the journey. هیچ is the negative quantifier 'none / never' that will negate what follows.
segments.s201-g70: بهر is a preposition 'for / toward' introducing the purpose 'for a stopping‑place' (منزلی here = 'a station/place to stop'). اسپ دیگر بسیچ is a poetic order meaning 'many other horses' (اسپ 'horse', دیگر 'other/another', بسیچ 'many/much' used as a quantifier). The whole phrase says no other horses were left for any station.
segments.s201-g71: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. هم is an additive/emphatic particle 'also / too'. زین نشان is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with that sign on his saddle' (زین 'saddle', نشان 'token/mark'); together they mean he bore that distinguishing token on his saddle.
segments.s201-g72: بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional compound meaning 'near / close to'. آن نامور is a nominal phrase 'that renowned (one)' with نامور 'renowned' qualifying آن. سرکشان here is a proper name or title (a leader/figure) used as the noun governed by the preceding phrase; its listed lexical senses do not match this usage so it is treated as a name/title in the context.
segments.s201-g97: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = from that; پس = after/then). بدو is a dative clitic phrase 'to him' (ب + دو = به + او), and گفت is the past tense 'he said' introducing reported speech. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, followed by بدنشان 'their bodies/persons' (بدن + شان) used here as an emphatic/pejorative vocative phrase.
segments.s201-g98: که here begins the content clause introduced by the reported speech ('that/that which'). کمباد is a poetic noun 'a light breeze'; نامت is 'your name/reputation' (نام + -ت); ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and گردنکشان ('neck-cutters' / rebels) is the source phrase. The clause is elliptic: the verb that would tie 'کمباد' and 'نامت' (e.g. 'carries'/'takes') is left implied in this poetic line.
segments.s201-g99: نترسی is a present/imperative-form addressing the second person ('do not fear' / 'you do not fear'); همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (here 'indeed' / 'ever'); از introduces the agent/source 'از جهاندار پاک' — 'from/about the pure sovereign' (جهاندار = sovereign, پاک = pure as an epithet placed after the noun).
segments.s201-g100: This clause has ز گردان 'because of/from the troop/host' as a circumstantial phrase. نیامد is used impersonally/predicatively 'did not become' or 'did not befit', and ترا is the dative/accusative cluster (ت = 2nd-person clitic + را object marker) meaning 'to you'. شرم و باک ('shame and fear') are the subjects/qualities that 'do not befit' you. Word order is poetic: circumstantial phrase — verb — dative — the qualities.
segments.s201-g101: نگفتم is the past negated first-person verb 'I did not say' (negation + گفتن), and it introduces a quoted imperative that follows. مرو here is the imperative 'do not go' (or the quoted negative imperative). سوی راه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the road/way', and چرم appears here as a place-name (poetic/toponymic); the phrase as a whole reports 'I did not say “Do not go toward the road of Chirm.”' Note: چرم's lexical senses given in the list (leather) do not cover its use as a place-name, so that sense is flagged below.
segments.s201-g102: برفتی is past second-person 'you went' (بر + رفتی), followed by و 'and'. دادی is past 'you gave/caused' and takes دل من 'my heart' as its object; به غم is a prepositional phrase 'to/into sorrow' showing the result/effect ('you caused my heart to sorrow'). Word order is straightforward: verb — conjunction — verb — object — prepositional result.
segments.s201-g103: نخستین is an adverb/adjective 'first' or 'at first'. بکین is an archaic/poetic form related to بکن (imperative or aorist-like 2nd-person form) used here in a narrative/performative sense; the sequence بکین من آراستی reads as 'you first adorned me' (من = me, آراستی = you adorned). The order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (adverb — verb — object — verb).
segments.s201-g104: نژاد سیاوش را is the direct object (نژاد = lineage/race; سیاوش = proper name; را = object marker). کاستی here functions as past second-person 'you diminished/reduced' (from کاستن) — an archaic past form used in poetry. The literal meaning: 'you lessened Siavash's lineage.' The provided lexical senses did not include the verbal reading of کاستی, so that sense is flagged below.
segments.s201-g57: سرافراز is an epithet placed before the proper name and functions as an adjectival epithet 'honored/illustrious.' گودرز is the proper name. ازان is the archaic contracted preposition + demonstrative 'from that / of that' and it links the person to the following noun انجمن 'assembly/meeting.' Word order is epithet — name — provenance.
segments.s201-g58: بهر کار = 'for the business/affair' (بهر = for; کار = affair). باشد here is the jussive/subjunctive 'let him be' (classical Persian optative: 'may he be'). ترا appears split in the word list as suffix ت + را and functions as the dative/benefactive 'for you' (= تو را). رای زن is a compound meaning 'rāy-zen' — 'one who gives counsel' (رای = counsel/opinion; زن is here from زدن used in compounds to mean 'give/do' so the phrase means 'counsel-giver' / 'adviser.'
segments.s201-g59: مکن is the classical negative imperative 'do not.' هیچ intensifies the prohibition ('at all/never'). در جنگ جستن is an infinitival phrase 'to seek/enter into battle' (در جنگ = in battle; جستن = the infinitive 'to seek/attempt'). شتاب is the noun 'haste' and here qualifies the prohibition — overall: 'Do not be hasty to seek battle.'
segments.s201-g60: ز = 'from / away from'. می‌دور (from دوریدن/دور زدن family) is used as 'to roam / wander' here, so ز می‌دور باش literally 'be away from roaming' = 'avoid wandering.' باش is the imperative 'be!' instructing the addressee. مپیمای is a negative subjunctive/imperative form (م- + پیمای) meaning 'do not traverse/venture' or idiomatically 'do not yield' and خواب 'sleep' is its object: 'do not let sleep come on you' or 'do not fall asleep.'
segments.s201-g61: بتندی is a verbal form meaning 'to hurry / to become swift' used here as the notion to be avoided. مجو is the prohibitive 'do not seek' (imperative negative). ایچ (ایچ/هیچ in older spelling) intensifies 'not at all' ('not/at all'). رزم = 'battle.' از نخست = 'from the first/at the outset.' Together: 'Do not be hasty in seeking battle from the very beginning.'
segments.s201-g62: همی باش is an exhortation 'remain so / continue thus' (همی = still/continue; باش = be/imperative). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until.' خسته گردد is subjunctive/passive-style 'becomes tired' (گردد = become). درست here works adverbially 'properly / thoroughly.' The clause means 'keep on until he becomes truly exhausted.'
segments.s201-g63: ترا here appears as the enclitic dative 'for you' (listed as ت + را) and marks the beneficiary. پیش رو is a compound meaning 'front / vanguard' (پیش = before; رو = face/toward). گیو is the proper name Giv. باشد is the jussive/subjunctive 'let him be.' بجنگ ('in/for battle' or 'to fight') is used adverbially: 'be (for) you at the front in battle.'
segments.s201-g64: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/for he.' با = 'with.' فر (farr) is the special royal/divine glory. و links items. برزست is a poetic/adjectival or verbal form meaning 'exalted/lofty' (here describing his might). و again 'and.' چنگ پلنگ = 'the claw (چنگ) of the leopard (پلنگ),' a figurative image of ferocity. The whole phrase lists Giv's qualities with 'with...' constructions.

sentences.s201-s53: A proud, warlike brother appeared — there had never been one like him in that age.
sentences.s201-s54: You killed a man who, on the battlefield, whenever you wished for troops for your affairs of war.
sentences.s201-s55: And after that, when you went to that battlefield, there remained for you nothing but repose and banqueting-halls.
sentences.s201-s56: You have no place in the city; fetters and the hospital befit you.
sentences.s201-s61: Go; the eternal house is your prison, and that very evil jewel is your guardian.
sentences.s201-s62: He drove him from his presence and commanded, 'Bind him'; bond by bond, they should tear the joy from the root of his heart.
sentences.s201-s9: No head finds release from his fetter; to one he grants all glory and splendour.
sentences.s201-s10: To another he gives misfortune; he bestows need, sorrow, pain, and hardship.
sentences.s201-s11: From the shining sun to the dark earth, I see all justice from the pure God.
sentences.s201-s12: Tūs, with the Kayanian standard, appeared (or was made); from the host came forty men with golden shoes.
sentences.s201-s45: He went off and carried away that ill-omened votive offering of the warlike horsemen and the troops.
sentences.s201-s46: He came close to the king from the battlefield; not acting like a mere porter, he did not delay in any way.
sentences.s201-s47: He kissed the ground before the king; Khosrow did not so much as look at him.
sentences.s201-s48: The ill‑famed man unloosed the king’s tongue; thereby he made Tūs despised in that assembly.
sentences.s201-s5: In the name of the Lord of the Sun and Moon; where did He place (or give) order upon the fair arrangement?
sentences.s201-s6: He created the world, place, and time.
sentences.s201-s7: From him are victory and patience; through good and ill (or by good and bad), from him come desire and beauty.
sentences.s201-s8: He bestowed wisdom, and a vigorous soul and body; greatness, rustic (or popular) force, and a lofty throne.
sentences.s201-s1: He summoned the wise secretary before him; his heart, filled with grief, let fall its tears.
sentences.s201-s2: He wrote a letter — one full of tears — for his brother, filled with pain and anger.
sentences.s201-s3: One (letter) to Fariburz, (son of) King Kavus; one toward the host of the nobles.
sentences.s201-s4: The opening of the letter began with praise of the First Creator, just as is the custom of ritual and religion.
sentences.s201-s25: All virtues are near him; may it not be that his soul is so dark.
sentences.s201-s26: When you read this letter, hurry at once and drive even a little calm and sleep far from your heart.
sentences.s201-s27: Return Sabak (of) Tus to his place; do not turn away from the command, and give no counsel.
sentences.s201-s28: Be commander and lord in golden boots; you shall be with the Kayanian standard.
sentences.s201-s37: The letter-bearer came to Fereyberz, and then gave him the king's letter.
sentences.s201-s38: Fereyberz summoned Tus and the heroes, and related at length the past deeds.
sentences.s201-s39: He summoned the famed Giv and Goudarz — the horsemen and the troops of that frontier.
sentences.s201-s40: When he read that royal letter aloud, a new tree sprang up in the world.
sentences.s201-s17: Now one must weep for the brother; I do not know who is enemy and who is friend to me.
sentences.s201-s18: “Do not go,” I said to him; do not strike toward Kalat and the Sepd‑mountain’s edge.
sentences.s201-s19: On that road they are lowly and are accompanied by a host; he is precisely the one whose lineage it is and who is an excavator.
sentences.s201-s20: They do not know from what root this host springs; if (it is) from Iran, the commander will gather his own (troops).
sentences.s201-s13: I sent him to Turan with an army; my brother was ruined by that first hatred.
sentences.s201-s14: May there be no such chief in Iran, and may there be no commander of the army of this weighty kind.
sentences.s201-s15: Alas! my brother fell when young — the chief of the renowned and the support (back) of his peers.
sentences.s201-s16: Because of my father's hatred I was wretched and weeping; for that sorrow I was burned/consumed for some time.
sentences.s201-s41: All the great men and brave warriors of the land of Iran cried out to the king, "Bravo!"
sentences.s201-s42: Tūs brought that honored standard (the 'Aba'), along with drums, elephants, and gilded shoes.
sentences.s201-s43: They brought [it] to Fariborz and said that a fitting recompense had come for the deserving pair.
sentences.s201-s44: May your fortune be victorious every year; may all your days be Nowruz.
sentences.s201-s21: From that mountain he brought war, without doubt; time will bring forth many leaders.
sentences.s201-s22: Alas for the scion of Khosrow — so it was that Tūs, base-born, cast him to the winds.
sentences.s201-s23: If, before this, he had been granted the rank of spahbed, by King Kāvus — ill-starred.
sentences.s201-s24: Even within the camp sleep comes to him; when he sits without (it), haste comes upon him.
sentences.s201-s57: You have no place before the nobles; nowhere is your counsel sound.
sentences.s201-s58: Worthy of a nail‑studded stake and bonds and fetters; not fit to partake of the crown, the rural holdings, and the realm.
sentences.s201-s59: The lineage of Manuchehr and a white beard have given you hope for life.
sentences.s201-s60: Otherwise I would have ordered it: you harbored ill intent and acted apart from your lord.
sentences.s201-s33: Raise the instruments of battle from every quarter; may it not be that the counsel of the banquet comes to you.
sentences.s201-s34: He placed the king's seal on the letter‑carrier; he told the messenger, 'Set out on the road.'
sentences.s201-s35: From travelling night and day toward Masāy, there was never another horse left for any stopping‑place.
sentences.s201-s36: The messenger also came, bearing that token on his saddle, to the renowned leader.
sentences.s201-s49: After that he said to him, 'O — of their persons — that a light breeze would carry your name from the neck-cutters.'
sentences.s201-s50: Do not fear, indeed, the pure sovereign; shame and fear do not become you because of the host.
sentences.s201-s51: I did not say 'Do not go toward the road of Chirm'; you went, and you gave my heart to grief.
sentences.s201-s52: First you (poetically) adorned me; you diminished the lineage of Siavash.
sentences.s201-s29: Let the honored Goudarz from that assembly be your adviser for the affair.
sentences.s201-s30: Do not at all rush to seek battle; keep away from roaming, and do not let sleep overtake you.
sentences.s201-s31: Do not be eager to rush into battle at the outset; keep on until he becomes thoroughly weary.
sentences.s201-s32: Let Giv be before you in battle as your vanguard, for he fights with royal glory, exalted strength, and the claw of a leopard.
